title: Singapore Maritime Decarbonisation Blueprint Implementation Regulations
full_brief:
# Singapore Maritime Decarbonisation Blueprint — Regulatory Fact Document

---

# 1. Purpose and Scope of This Document

This document analyses the Maritime Singapore Decarbonisation Blueprint ("the Blueprint"), published in 2022 by the Maritime and Port Authority of Singapore (MPA), and its active implementation measures as updated through 2024–2025. It covers the Blueprint's seven focus areas, associated incentive frameworks under the Maritime Singapore Green Initiative (MSGI), harbour craft regulatory mandates taking effect from 2030, and Singapore's advancing alternative-fuel bunkering regulatory programme.

**Audience.** This brief is written for legal counsel, operations leads, and commercial leadership at a freight-forwarding workspace operating primarily by air, with ocean as a secondary or tertiary mode, across Singapore-proximate and Asia-Pacific trade lanes.

**Convention notes.** The Blueprint is a strategic policy document, not enacted primary legislation. Its targets and timelines represent stated government ambition. Specific compliance mandates flow from subsequent regulations, port circulars, and licensing conditions issued under Singaporean maritime law. Every sentence in this document using a binding modal verb is either (a) a span-grounded FACT citing the source text, (b) labeled as *Analytical inference:*, *Operational implication:*, or *Industry interpretation:*, or (c) routed to a *Legal Confirmation Required:* callout. Unlabeled binding assertions are not permitted.

**Primary source note.** The MPA URL nominated as the primary source (https://mpa.gov.sg/media-centre/details/mpa-releases-detailed-implementation-plan-for-maritime-decarbonisation) returned a 404 error. No content was retrievable from that URL. All FACT-class claims in this document are grounded in the six alternative source blocks confirmed as accessible. The missing primary URL is flagged as a gap throughout.

**Dates.** Blueprint published: 9 March 2022. This brief generated: June 2026. All "as of" references use June 2026 unless stated otherwise.

---

# 2. What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies to the Workspace

**What the Blueprint is.** The Maritime Singapore Decarbonisation Blueprint: Working Towards 2050 is the MPA's overarching strategic framework for decarbonising Singapore's maritime sector. It was "developed by the Maritime and Port Authority of Singapore (MPA) in consultation with industry partners" and "takes on board the recommendations made by the International Advisory Panel on Maritime Decarbonisation, and inputs from a two-month long public consultation and sector-specific consultations." *Source: MPA Blueprint Page, MPA, 2022. https://www.mpa.gov.sg/maritime-singapore/sustainability/maritime-singapore-decarbonisation-blueprint

**Jurisdictional scope.** The Blueprint applies to: Singapore-registered ships (SRS fleet), harbour craft operating in Singapore waters, ocean-going vessels calling at the Port of Singapore, and Singapore-based maritime businesses. *Source: COS 2022 Media Factsheet, MPA, 9 March 2022. https://www.mpa.gov.sg/media-centre/details/cos-2022---media-factsheet---maritime-singapore-decarbonisation-blueprint-working-towards-2050

**Effective date.** The Blueprint was published on 9 March 2022. Implementation measures are phased, with key operational mandates taking effect from 2025 (MSGI incentive refreshes) and 2030 (harbour craft mandates). *Source: COS 2022 Media Factsheet, MPA, 9 March 2022. https://www.mpa.gov.sg/media-centre/details/cos-2022---media-factsheet---maritime-singapore-decarbonisation-blueprint-working-towards-2050

**Why it applies to the workspace.** For freight-forwarding workspaces moving cargo through the Port of Singapore — whether as shipper, forwarder, or logistics intermediary — the Blueprint creates the policy environment shaping vessel availability, bunkering costs, port surcharges, and green-shipping client expectations across Singapore-proximate trade lanes. The ocean mode implications are direct: vessels calling at Singapore will face an evolving incentive and regulatory structure affecting bunker fuel types, port dues, and green certification. *Analytical inference:* Workspaces whose ocean freight moves through Singapore will encounter these requirements indirectly through carrier pricing and vessel selection decisions.

**Penalty provision.** *Specific penalty/enforcement provision not available from primary sources as of June 2026.* The Blueprint is a strategic policy document; individual enforcement penalties flow from specific subsidiary regulations, port circulars, and licensing conditions. The accessible source blocks do not contain penalty figures applicable to freight forwarders or shippers. This is an explicit gap.

---

# 3. Issues Requiring Immediate Action

**MONITORING — Green Ship Programme incentive window open through 31 December 2027.**
Ocean-going vessels calling at Singapore are currently eligible for up to 100% port dues concession based on fuel type. The incentive window runs "from 1 January 2025 until 31 December 2027." *Source: MSGI Page, MPA, 2024. https://www.mpa.gov.sg/maritime-singapore/sustainability/maritime-singapore-green-initiative Workspaces whose ocean carriers call at Singapore should confirm with carriers whether they are accessing this concession and whether any Green Ship Programme (GSP) savings are being passed through in freight rates. This is a commercial tracking action, not a compliance action.

**MONITORING — Harbour craft 2030 mandate: charging and fueling infrastructure decisions in planning phase.**
The SSA-MPA September 2025 briefing confirmed that "from 2030 onwards, all new harbour crafts must meet one of three criteria: they must be fully electric, compatible with B100 biofuel, or net-zero fuels (NZFs) ready." *Source: MPA-SSA Briefing, SSA, 11 September 2025. https://www.ssa.org.sg/mpa-ssa-briefing-unites-harbour-craft-stakeholders-to-meet-decarbonisation-goals/ For workspaces that use harbour craft services (e.g., lighterage, barge transfer, port support) in Singapore operations, the 2030 mandate will affect the craft fleet serving them. MPA is "exploring measures for low/zero-emission harbour crafts to lower their costs" and has "shared preliminary plans for charging infrastructure." *Source: MPA-SSA Briefing, SSA, 11 September 2025.* Cost implications are not yet quantified in public sources.

**MONITORING — Methanol bunkering regulatory framework: under active development.**
The EOI process completed in April 2024 produced 50 submissions. "Insights from the EOI submissions will inform the development of the methanol bunkering regulatory framework to ensure the safe and efficient supply of methanol." *Source: Singapore Gears Up, MPA, 17 April 2024. https://www.mpa.gov.sg/media-centre/details/singapore-gears-up-to-meet-net-zero-needs-of-shipping The specific regulatory framework has not yet been published as of the accessible source blocks (April 2024 is the latest dated release). Workspaces managing ocean freight contracts that include fuel-type provisions should flag this as a re-check item for late 2025 / early 2026.

**MONITORING — MPA decarbonisation taskforce formation (September 2025).**
"MPA will form a decarbonisation taskforce to shape and co-develop policies and practical solutions with industry partners." *Source: MPA-SSA Briefing, SSA, 11 September 2025.* Taskforce outputs could include harbour craft transition support measures, financing frameworks, and revised port operating requirements — all of which would affect logistics service providers using Singapore port operations.

---

# 4. How the Workspace Sits in the Compliance Chain

The Blueprint primarily targets: vessel operators (ocean-going and harbour craft), port terminal operators, Singapore Registry of Ships registrants, and Singapore-based maritime businesses.

For a freight-forwarding workspace, the compliance chain position is as follows:

- **As freight forwarder/logistics intermediary:** Not a direct obligated party under the Blueprint's vessel, port, or registry-facing measures. *Analytical inference:* Obligations flow to carriers and vessel operators; the workspace's exposure is indirect, through carrier cost pass-through, vessel availability, and port surcharge mechanisms.
- **As shipper tendering ocean freight contracts:** The workspace's cargo moves on vessels that are subject to Singapore port dues structures, GSP incentives, and eventual harbour craft mandates. *Analytical inference:* Carriers that have accessed GSP port dues concessions may have lower operating costs on Singapore port calls; contract negotiation should factor this in.
- **As user of harbour craft services in Singapore:** Lighterage, barge transfer, or anchorage support services use harbour craft. The 2030 mandate covers new harbour craft; existing craft are subject to transition timelines still being developed. *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether any pass-through cost obligation to cargo owners or forwarders arises from harbour craft fleet transition costs requires Singapore maritime law counsel review.
- **As a Singapore Registry of Ships (SRS) participant:** *Not applicable unless the workspace owns or manages vessels registered in Singapore.* The SRS green ship programme is relevant only for ship operators/owners.

*Source: COS 2022 Media Factsheet, MPA, 9 March 2022. https://www.mpa.gov.sg/media-centre/details/cos-2022---media-factsheet---maritime-singapore-decarbonisation-blueprint-working-towards-2050
*Source: MSGI Page, MPA, 2024. https://www.mpa.gov.sg/maritime-singapore/sustainability/maritime-singapore-green-initiative

---

# 5. Authoritative Guidance Document Analysis

*No single consolidated implementation regulation or guidance document is accessible from the provided source blocks. The primary URL (https://mpa.gov.sg/media-centre/details/mpa-releases-detailed-implementation-plan-for-maritime-decarbonisation) returned 404. Specific implementation is carried through individual port circulars (e.g., Shipping Circular No. 12 of 2024, Port Marine Circular No. 13 of 2024, Port Marine Circular No. 15 of 2024), which are referenced in the MSGI source block but whose text is not available in the provided source pool.*

The following analysis is drawn from the confirmed accessible source blocks:

**Blueprint focus area table (grounded FACT).** The Blueprint "outlines seven key areas which MPA will focus on to support the decarbonisation of the maritime industry," with specific targets per area as follows:

- Port terminals: "At least 60% reduction in absolute emissions from 2005 levels by 2030; Net zero emissions by 2050."
- Domestic harbour craft: "All harbour craft will operate on low-carbon energy solutions by 2030 and full electric propulsion and net zero fuels by 2050," targeting "15% reduction in absolute emissions from 2021 levels by 2030; 50% reduction in absolute emissions from 2030 levels by 2050."
- Future marine fuels: readiness for "multi-fuel bunkering transition" covering "biofuels, methanol, ammonia, and potentially hydrogen."
- Singapore Registry of Ships: "50% of the SRS fleet to be green ships by 2050."
- IMO and international platforms: standard-setting and advocacy role.
- R&D and talent: global hub positioning.
- Carbon awareness, accounting, and green financing: green maritime finance hub.

*Source: COS 2022 Media Factsheet, MPA, 9 March 2022. https://www.mpa.gov.sg/media-centre/details/cos-2022---media-factsheet---maritime-singapore-decarbonisation-blueprint-working-towards-2050

**MSGI 2024 refresh (grounded FACT).** "In 2024, MPA updated various green initiatives and incentives under the MSGI to encourage early adoption of zero and near-zero emission technologies and fuels. MPA will commit another S$50 million to support the implementation of the refreshed MSGI." *Source: MSGI Page, MPA, 2024. https://www.mpa.gov.sg/maritime-singapore/sustainability/maritime-singapore-green-initiative

**GSP port dues concession tiers (grounded FACT).** The Green Ship Programme provides up to 100% port dues concession for ocean-going vessels calling at Port of Singapore, with concession levels tiered by fuel type: zero-emission fuel/technology at the top tier; zero-carbon fuels (ammonia with conditions, B100 biofuel, green methanol); low-carbon fuels with CF value ≤ 1.375 (e.g., methanol), or LNG with methane slip addressed; and low-carbon fuels with CF value between 1.375 and 2.750 (e.g., LNG without methane slip addressed, biofuels B24–B49). *Source: MSGI Page, MPA, 2024. https://www.mpa.gov.sg/maritime-singapore/sustainability/maritime-singapore-green-initiative

*Analytical inference:* The tiered concession structure creates a direct financial incentive hierarchy for carriers calling at Singapore, with the largest concessions available to the cleanest fuel types. Workspaces tendering Singapore port calls in ocean freight contracts should request carrier disclosure of their GSP tier status as this affects operating cost basis.

---

# 6. Anticipated Authoritative Guidance and Pending Regulatory Events

**Methanol bunkering regulatory framework — publication pending, timeline not stated.** The April 2024 MPA release states that EOI insights "will inform the development of the methanol bunkering regulatory framework." *Source: Singapore Gears Up, MPA, 17 April 2024. https://www.mpa.gov.sg/media-centre/details/singapore-gears-up-to-meet-net-zero-needs-of-shipping No specific publication date is stated in the available source blocks. *Analytical inference:* Given that 50 EOI submissions were received in April 2024 and methanol bunker tanker deliveries are described as scheduled for 2024, regulatory framework publication is likely within a 12–24 month window from April 2024. Re-check: June–December 2025. **Severity: MONITORING.**

**Ammonia bunkering standards — pre-FEED milestones reached as of April 2024.** The SMW 2024 release references ammonia bunkering pre-FEED milestones. *Specific regulatory timeline for ammonia bunkering framework not available from primary sources as of June 2026.* No published date for ammonia bunkering regulation was confirmed in the accessible source blocks. **Severity: MONITORING.**

**Harbour craft transition support measures — MPA decarbonisation taskforce.** As of September 2025, "MPA will form a decarbonisation taskforce to shape and co-develop policies and practical solutions with industry partners." *Source: MPA-SSA Briefing, SSA, 11 September 2025. https://www.ssa.org.sg/mpa-ssa-briefing-unites-harbour-craft-stakeholders-to-meet-decarbonisation-goals/ Taskforce outputs — including cost-reduction measures, charging infrastructure plans, and possible financing instruments — are expected ahead of the 2030 mandate. No specific taskforce output publication date is stated. *Analytical inference:* Given the 2030 mandate for new harbour craft, taskforce outputs are expected between 2025 and 2028. Re-check: mid-2026. **Severity: MONITORING.**

**IMO mid-term GHG measures — adoption targeted late 2025.** At the April 2024 conference, IMO Secretary-General Arsenio Dominguez stated: "We are on track to adopt mid-term measures by late 2025 to cut GHG emissions." The measures described are "a goal-based marine fuel standard, and a pricing mechanism for maritime GHG emissions." *Source: Singapore Gears Up, MPA, 17 April 2024. https://www.mpa.gov.sg/media-centre/details/singapore-gears-up-to-meet-net-zero-needs-of-shipping IMO GHG measures, once adopted, would directly affect all ocean carriers using Singapore as a bunkering and port-call location — and by cost pass-through, freight pricing on all trade lanes through Singapore. **Severity: MONITORING.** Re-check: Q1 2026 for IMO adoption status.

**MSGI Green Ship Programme — window expiry 31 December 2027.** The current GSP incentive period runs through 31 December 2027. *Source: MSGI Page, MPA, 2024.* Whether the programme is renewed or replaced by mandatory measures has not been stated in available sources. **Severity: WINDOW CLOSING** (for carriers and vessel operators; indirect for freight forwarders).

---

# 7. Threshold Questions

| Question | Status |
|---|---|
| Does the Blueprint create direct obligations on freight forwarders or cargo owners? | *Legal Confirmation Required:* The Blueprint targets vessel operators, port operators, and maritime businesses. Direct freight-forwarder obligations are not visible in the accessible sources, but subsequent port circulars and licensing conditions may impose indirect requirements. Counsel review of current MPA port operating conditions recommended. |
| Are harbour craft used by the workspace's Singapore operations subject to the 2030 mandate? | The 2030 mandate covers "new harbour crafts" from 2030. *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether existing craft used by or contracted to the workspace are subject to retrofit or transition obligations before 2030 requires review of specific craft licensing conditions. |
| Do the GSP port dues concessions apply to the workspace's ocean carriers? | The concession applies to ocean-going vessels calling at Port of Singapore. *Analytical inference:* This is a carrier-level benefit; the workspace benefits only if carriers pass through savings in freight rates. Confirmed only by direct carrier inquiry. |
| Is the workspace's Singapore-based operation eligible for any MSGI sub-programme? | The Green Energy and Technology Programme (GETP) and Green Awareness Programme (GAP) are available to "Singapore-based maritime companies." *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether a freight-forwarding operation qualifies as a "Singapore-based maritime company" for MSGI eligibility requires MPA confirmation. |

---

# 8. Substantive Requirements

## 8.1 Port Terminals

The Blueprint sets a target of "at least 60% reduction in absolute emissions from 2005 levels by 2030" and "Net zero emissions by 2050" for port terminals. *Source: COS 2022 Media Factsheet, MPA, 9 March 2022. https://www.mpa.gov.sg/media-centre/details/cos-2022---media-factsheet---maritime-singapore-decarbonisation-blueprint-working-towards-2050

*Operational implication:* Terminal decarbonisation programmes — including electrification of handling equipment — will affect port handling charges at Singapore's container and general cargo terminals over the medium term. The direction of effect (higher infrastructure costs offset by lower energy costs) is not yet quantified in the available source blocks.

## 8.2 Domestic Harbour Craft

The stated ambition is that "all harbour craft will operate on low-carbon energy solutions by 2030 and full electric propulsion and net zero fuels by 2050." *Source: COS 2022 Media Factsheet, MPA, 9 March 2022.*

The SSA-MPA September 2025 briefing operationalises the 2030 threshold: "from 2030 onwards, all new harbour crafts must meet one of three criteria: they must be fully electric, compatible with B100 biofuel, or net-zero fuels (NZFs) ready." *Source: MPA-SSA Briefing, SSA, 11 September 2025. https://www.ssa.org.sg/mpa-ssa-briefing-unites-harbour-craft-stakeholders-to-meet-decarbonisation-goals/

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces relying on Singapore harbour craft services — including any lighterage, barge transfer, or anchorage support used in ocean freight operations — the 2030 new-craft mandate will reshape the available vessel fleet. Existing craft transition timelines are still being determined through the MPA decarbonisation taskforce. Cost implications are unquantified in current public sources.

The Green Craft Programme (GCP) under MSGI provides port dues concessions for newbuild harbour craft meeting zero-emission, zero-carbon, or low-carbon fuel criteria, registered from 1 January 2025 through 31 December 2027. *Source: MSGI Page, MPA, 2024. https://www.mpa.gov.sg/maritime-singapore/sustainability/maritime-singapore-green-initiative

## 8.3 Future Marine Fuels and Bunkering

**Methanol.** Following "the world's first ship-to-containership methanol bunkering in Singapore," MPA launched an EOI for methanol supply. The EOI received 50 submissions; "the demand for methanol as a marine fuel in Singapore can potentially exceed one million tons per annum (MTPA) before 2030." Submitted proposals "have the potential to supply over one MTPA of low-carbon methanol by 2030, subject to commercial decisions and global developments." *Source: Singapore Gears Up, MPA, 17 April 2024. https://www.mpa.gov.sg/media-centre/details/singapore-gears-up-to-meet-net-zero-needs-of-shipping

**Ammonia.** Ammonia bunkering pre-FEED milestones are referenced in the April 2024 MPA release. *Specific regulatory timeline for ammonia bunkering not available from primary sources as of June 2026.*

**GSP fuel-type tiers.** The current GSP applies the following concession hierarchy for ocean-going vessels at Port of Singapore (1 January 2025 – 31 December 2027):

| Tier | Fuel/Technology | Concession |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Zero-emission (hydrogen, full electrification, hydrogen fuel cell/electrification hybrid) | Up to 100% port dues |
| 2 | Zero-carbon (ammonia with pilot fuel ≤25%, B100 biofuel, green methanol) | Up to 100% port dues |
| 3 | Low-carbon CF ≤ 1.375 (e.g., methanol); LNG with methane slip addressed to max 1%; biofuels B50–B99 | Up to 100% port dues |
| 4 | Low-carbon 1.375 < CF ≤ 2.750 (e.g., LNG without methane slip addressed; biofuels B24–B49) | Up to 100% port dues |

*Source: MSGI Page, MPA, 2024. https://www.mpa.gov.sg/maritime-singapore/sustainability/maritime-singapore-green-initiative

*Analytical inference:* The multi-tier structure signals that Singapore is building toward a fuel-standard differentiated port pricing model. When incentive concessions expire (currently 31 December 2027), the precedent structure could transition to mandatory tiered port dues. Workspaces with long-term ocean freight contracts on Singapore calls should consider asking carriers to disclose their GSP tier position and contractual treatment of any future mandatory fees.

## 8.4 Singapore Registry of Ships (SRS)

The Blueprint targets "50% of the SRS fleet to be green ships by 2050." The GSP provides "up to 100% concessions on initial registration fees and annual tonnage taxes for SRS" meeting specified criteria (1 January 2025 – 31 December 2027). *Source: MSGI Page, MPA, 2024; COS 2022 Media Factsheet, MPA, 9 March 2022.*

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces that do not own or manage SRS-registered vessels, this focus area has no direct compliance relevance. Its indirect relevance is commercial: SRS registration incentives affect the economics of Singapore-flagged vessels used by carriers on trade lanes relevant to the workspace.

## 8.5 Carbon Accounting and Green Financing

The Blueprint identifies "green maritime finance hub" status as a target under Focus Area 7, with carbon accounting and reporting as a central activity. The Green Awareness Programme (GAP) under MSGI focuses on "raising awareness on and developing industry capabilities in carbon accounting and management" through a Carbon Management MoU signed between MPA, UN Global Compact Network Singapore, and industry associations. *Source: MSGI Page, MPA, 2024.*

*Analytical inference:* As Singapore strengthens carbon accounting infrastructure for the maritime sector, freight forwarders operating through Singapore may encounter carrier requests for Scope 3 emissions data or face client expectations around verified maritime carbon accounting. This connects to international disclosure frameworks (ISO 14083, CSRD Scope 3) relevant to the workspace's global reporting obligations.

---

# 9. Product-Specific Compliance Status

*No content for this section as of June 2026: this section applies when the workspace sells products directly in scope of the regulation. The Blueprint targets vessels, port operators, and maritime businesses — not specific freight products. No product-specific compliance status applies to a freight-forwarding workspace under this framework.*

---

# 10. Registration and Reporting Obligations

**MSGI programme registration.** The GSP, GCP, and GETP are described as "voluntary programmes designed to recognise and provide incentives to companies that adopt clean and green shipping practices." *Source: MSGI Page, MPA, 2024.* Registration is voluntary; no mandatory registration obligation on freight forwarders is identified in the available sources.

**SRS Green Notation.** The COS 2022 factsheet references "SRS Green Notation" as part of Blueprint Focus Area 4. This is a voluntary notation for Singapore-registered vessels. *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether any reporting obligation attaches to cargo owners or forwarders using SRS-flagged vessels with Green Notation status requires MPA clarification.

**IMO GHG reporting (indirect).** Singapore vessels are subject to IMO MARPOL Annex VI fuel oil consumption data collection requirements. These obligations sit with vessel operators, not freight forwarders. *Analytical inference:* However, as Scope 3 reporting frameworks (CSRD, GHG Protocol) increasingly require freight customers to report carrier emissions, carrier IMO fuel data will become a source that workspaces should request access to in commercial contracts.

*Specific gap: No mandatory reporting obligation on freight forwarders or shippers was identified in the available source blocks as of June 2026.*

---

# 11. Operational System Requirements

## Air-Priority Operations
*No content for this section: the Blueprint applies exclusively to maritime operations. Air freight operations are not in scope.*

## Ocean-Mode Operations (secondary mode for the workspace)

**Carrier screening for GSP tier.** *Operational implication:* Workspaces tendering ocean freight on Singapore port-call routes should add GSP tier status as a carrier due-diligence data point. Carriers on Tier 1–3 fuels have a lower port dues cost base, which should be visible in rate competitiveness.

**Contract clause readiness for fuel surcharges.** *Analytical inference:* As the GSP incentive window closes at end-2027 and as IMO mid-term GHG pricing measures approach potential adoption, ocean freight contracts covering 2026–2028 on Singapore routes should include provisions for fuel surcharge pass-through transparency and caps. No specific clause format is mandated by the Blueprint; this is a commercial risk management action.

**Harbour craft service continuity planning.** For Singapore port operations involving harbour craft (lighterage, crew transfer, anchorage support), the 2030 new-craft mandate and the pending transition support framework create uncertainty about craft availability and cost during 2026–2030. *Operational implication:* Workspaces should ask Singapore port agents and logistics partners to report on their harbour craft operators' transition status when the MPA taskforce outputs are published.

**Gap from current operational baseline.** The workspace's ocean-mode operations are likely managed through standard carrier contracts without specific fuel-type or sustainability data clauses. The gap between current state and the emerging requirement is: (a) no GSP-tier carrier data; (b) no fuel surcharge transparency clause; (c) no harbour craft transition monitoring process. Each is addressable through contract amendment and procurement policy update, not capital investment. *Analytical inference:* These are low-cost operational adjustments; the higher-cost exposure is if IMO GHG pricing converts from voluntary to mandatory, which would be a COST ALERT event requiring a separate brief.

---

# 12. Exemptions and Edge Cases

*No content for this section as of June 2026: the available source blocks do not specify exemption provisions applicable to freight-forwarding workspaces. MSGI programmes are voluntary; no exemption framework from a mandatory obligation is identified for this workspace profile. Legal counsel should review whether any future harbour craft transition obligations contain small-fleet or existing-craft exemptions when MPA taskforce outputs are published.*

---

# 13. Adjacent Industry Research and Alternatives

**IMO 2023 GHG Strategy checkpoints.** At the April 2024 event, IMO Secretary-General Dominguez stated IMO GHG Strategy targets: "to reach net zero targets" with "indicative checkpoints to reach by 2030 (cut GHG emissions by at least 20%, striving for 30%), and 2040 (cut GHG emissions by at least 70%, striving for 80%)." *Source: Singapore Gears Up, MPA, 17 April 2024. https://www.mpa.gov.sg/media-centre/details/singapore-gears-up-to-meet-net-zero-needs-of-shipping

*Analytical inference:* These IMO checkpoints are the global framework within which Singapore's Blueprint sits. The Blueprint's harbour craft and port terminal targets are aligned with but more specific than IMO-level commitments. For workspaces tracking ocean freight sustainability at the global level, the IMO mid-term measure adoption (targeted late 2025) is the upstream event that will determine whether Singapore's voluntary incentives convert into compliance requirements.

**Methanol supply chain development.** MPA's April 2024 release indicates "several projects already in operation or have crossed Final Investment Decision" for low-carbon methanol supply, with aggregate potential "to supply over one MTPA of low-carbon methanol by 2030." *Source: Singapore Gears Up, MPA, 17 April 2024.* This is an industry supply chain development finding, not a regulatory requirement. *Analytical inference:* If methanol supply scales as projected, carriers selecting Singapore as a methanol bunkering hub will have lower blended fuel costs by 2030 compared to ports without methanol infrastructure — a competitive advantage for Singapore-routed ocean freight.

**Workforce reskilling.** The Workforce Singapore Career Conversion Programme for Sea Transport "offers salary support for reskilling local talent in green vessel operations," with focus on "IoT fleet management and predictive maintenance" and "carbon accounting and battery systems management." *Source: MPA-SSA Briefing, SSA, 11 September 2025. https://www.ssa.org.sg/mpa-ssa-briefing-unites-harbour-craft-stakeholders-to-meet-decarbonisation-goals/ *Analytical inference:* Availability of this programme signals that Singapore is investing in the labour supply needed to operate new vessel types. This reduces the workforce availability risk for the harbour craft transition, which is a relevant operational continuity factor for workspaces using harbour craft services.

---

# 14. Confirmed Regulatory Timeline

| Date | Milestone | Status | Workspace Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| 9 March 2022 | Blueprint published by MPA | In force as of 9 March 2022 | Policy framework established; monitoring begins |
| 2022–2024 | MSGI enhancements made (2013, 2019, 2022 cycles); 2024 refresh adds S$50M commitment | In force | Voluntary incentive programmes available to eligible Singapore maritime companies |
| 1 January 2025 | Green Ship Programme and Green Craft Programme refresh takes effect | In force as of 1 January 2025 | Ocean carriers on Singapore calls eligible for tiered port dues concessions; workspaces should confirm carrier GSP status |
| 31 March 2027 | EEG base-tier application window closes | Future deadline | Relevant to Singapore-based port/logistics businesses investing in energy-efficient equipment |
| 31 December 2027 | GSP and GCP incentive period expires | Future deadline | Post-2027 incentive structure not yet announced; WINDOW CLOSING for voluntary scheme |
| Late 2025 (IMO target) | IMO mid-term GHG measures adoption (goal-based fuel standard + GHG pricing mechanism) | Pending; stated target | If adopted, converts global ocean shipping fuel economics — would require separate brief |
| 2030 | All new harbour craft in Singapore must be fully electric, B100-compatible, or NZF-ready | Future mandate | Affects harbour craft fleet serving Singapore port operations; MPA taskforce developing transition support |
| 2030 | Port terminals target: 60% absolute emissions reduction from 2005 levels | Target | Terminal handling cost trajectory; not a direct forwarder compliance deadline |
| 2030 | Harbour craft: 15% absolute emissions reduction from 2021 levels | Target | Intermediate fleet transition milestone |
| 2050 | Net zero: port terminals, SRS fleet (50% green ships), harbour craft (full electric/net zero fuels) | Long-term target | Strategic horizon; intermediate regulatory actions will define the path |

*Sources: COS 2022 Media Factsheet, MPA, 9 March 2022; MSGI Page, MPA, 2024; MPA-SSA Briefing, SSA, 11 September 2025; Singapore Gears Up, MPA, 17 April 2024.*

---
